{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_017ZSV59tons82VnwnNh6Pih","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"The user wants me to provide a clinical pharmacogenomics recommendation for tobramycin and the MT-RNR1 gene, specifically for a patient with \"normal risk of aminoglycoside-induced hearing loss\" genotype.\n\nLet me recall what I know about this drug-gene combination:\n\n**MT-RNR1 and Aminoglycoside-Induced Hearing Loss:**\n\nThe MT-RNR1 gene encodes the 12S ribosomal RNA (rRNA) in mitochondria. Certain variants in this gene, particularly the m.1555A>G and m.1494C>T variants, are associated with increased susceptibility to aminoglycoside-induced ototoxicity (hearing loss).\n\n\nThe mechanism involves aminoglycosides like tobramycin binding to mitochondrial ribosomes in patients carrying these variants, since the altered ribosome structure resembles bacterial ribosomes more closely. This increases mitochondrial toxicity in cochlear hair cells and can cause hearing loss even at standard therapeutic doses. For a patient with \"normal risk\" genotype, they don't carry these high-risk variants, so standard tobramycin dosing and monitoring protocols apply without the heightened ototoxicity concern. -risk variants substantially increase ototoxicity risk, and normal risk means standard guidelines apply. CPIC guidelines support using standard dosing with routine monitoring for patients with normal MT-RNR1 genotypes, so I'll recommend tobramycin per standard protocols without dose adjustment or antibiotic substitution needed based on this genetic result alone.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":3,"cache_creation_input_tokens":1193,"cache_read_input_tokens":18420,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":1193,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":8,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"global"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"c9ca8f60-43e9-4fe6-8c84-b0da62a71925","uuid":"14438d78-f797-4fe0-9026-5dd43e6ca3f9"}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_013f3Qoi8XjGTcUNwbuk3NFE","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The file is written and contains valid JSON. Here is a summary of the clinical reasoning:\n\n**MT-RNR1 & Aminoglycoside Ototoxicity Background**\n\nThe MT-RNR1 gene encodes mitochondrial 12S rRNA. High-risk variants — most notably **m.1555A>G** and **m.1494C>T** — structurally alter this rRNA so it resembles bacterial rRNA, making it a target for aminoglycoside binding. This leads to mitochondrial dysfunction in cochlear hair cells and irreversible sensorineural hearing loss, even at standard therapeutic doses.\n\n**For this patient (normal risk genotype)**\n\n| Field | Value |\n|---|---|\n| Recommendation | Use tobramycin per standard dosing; routine TDM and audiological monitoring apply |\n| Classification | **Strong** |\n| Implication | No elevated genetic risk for aminoglycoside-induced ototoxicity; general population baseline risk only |\n\nThe \"Strong\" classification reflects that the CPIC guideline for aminoglycosides and MT-RNR1 is well-established — the absence of high-risk variants clearly supports standard use without genetic-based modification. Non-genetic ototoxicity risk factors (cumulative dose, renal function, concurrent ototoxins) still warrant standard clinical vigilance."}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":579,"cache_read_input_tokens":20962,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":579,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"global"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"c9ca8f60-43e9-4fe6-8c84-b0da62a71925","uuid":"e1abddb6-93b9-4717-a5d7-be7ca776c458"}
